2	In this directory contains an example for generating a spice output using two
3	different spice modes, normal analog transient simulation and event-driven
4	digital simulation as supported by ngspice xspice sub-module.
5	
6	Each test bench can be run separately by either running:
7	
8	- testbench.sh, to start analog simulation or
9	- testbench_digital.sh for mixed-signal digital simulation.
10	
11	The later case also includes pure verilog simulation using the iverilog
12	and gtkwave for comparison.
13

file.content:
In this directory contains an example for generating a spice output using two
different spice modes, normal analog transient simulation and event-driven
digital simulation as supported by ngspice xspice sub-module.

Each test bench can be run separately by either running:

- testbench.sh, to start analog simulation or
- testbench_digital.sh for mixed-signal digital simulation.

The later case also includes pure verilog simulation using the iverilog
and gtkwave for comparison.

content.content:
1	Synthesis starter
2	-----------------
3	
4	This page will be a guided walkthrough of the prepackaged iCE40 FPGA synthesis
5	script - `synth_ice40`.  We will take a simple design through each step, looking
6	at the commands being called and what they do to the design. While `synth_ice40`
7	is specific to the iCE40 platform, most of the operations we will be discussing
8	are common across the majority of FPGA synthesis scripts. Thus, this document
9	will provide a good foundational understanding of how synthesis in Yosys is
10	performed, regardless of the actual architecture being used.
11	
12	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
13	   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/synth`
14	
15	Demo design
16	~~~~~~~~~~~
17	
18	.. role:: yoscrypt(code)
19	   :language: yoscrypt
20	
21	First, let's quickly look at the design we'll be synthesizing:
22	
23	.. todo:: reconsider including the whole (~77 line) design like this
24	
25	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.v
26	   :language: Verilog
27	   :linenos:
28	   :caption: :file:`fifo.v`
29	   :name: fifo-v
30	
31	.. todo:: fifo.v description
32	
33	While the open source `read_verilog` frontend generally does a pretty good job
34	at processing valid Verilog input, it does not provide very good error handling
35	or reporting.  Using an external tool such as `verilator`_ before running Yosys
36	is highly recommended. We can quickly check the Verilog syntax of our design by
37	calling ``verilator --lint-only fifo.v``.
38	
39	.. _verilator: https://www.veripool.org/verilator/
40	
41	Loading the design
42	~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
43	
44	Let's load the design into Yosys.  From the command line, we can call ``yosys
45	fifo.v``.  This will open an interactive Yosys shell session and immediately
46	parse the code from :ref:`fifo-v` and convert it into an Abstract Syntax Tree
47	(AST).  If you are interested in how this happens, there is more information in
48	the document, :doc:`/yosys_internals/flow/verilog_frontend`.  For now, suffice
49	it to say that we do this to simplify further processing of the design.  You
50	should see something like the following:
51	
52	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
53	   :language: console
54	   :start-at: $ yosys fifo.v
55	   :end-before: echo on
56	
57	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
58	   :doc:`/using_yosys/more_scripting/load_design`
59	
60	Elaboration
61	~~~~~~~~~~~
62	
63	Now that we are in the interactive shell, we can call Yosys commands directly.
64	Our overall goal is to call :yoscrypt:`synth_ice40 -top fifo`, but for now we
65	can run each of the commands individually for a better sense of how each part
66	contributes to the flow.  We will also start with just a single module;
67	``addr_gen``.
68	
69	At the bottom of the `help` output for
70	`synth_ice40` is the complete list of commands called by this script.
71	Let's start with the section labeled ``begin``:
72	
73	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
74	   :language: yoscrypt
75	   :start-after: begin:
76	   :end-before: flatten:
77	   :dedent:
78	   :caption: ``begin`` section
79	   :name: synth_begin
80	
81	:yoscrypt:`read_verilog -D ICE40_HX -lib -specify +/ice40/cells_sim.v` loads the
82	iCE40 cell models which allows us to include platform specific IP blocks in our
83	design.  PLLs are a common example of this, where we might need to reference
84	``SB_PLL40_CORE`` directly rather than being able to rely on mapping passes
85	later.  Since our simple design doesn't use any of these IP blocks, we can skip
86	this command for now.  Because these cell models will also be needed once we
87	start mapping to hardware we will still need to load them later.
88	
89	.. note::
90	
91	   ``+/`` is a dynamic reference to the Yosys ``share`` directory.  By default,
92	   this is ``/usr/local/share/yosys``.  If using a locally built version of
93	   Yosys from the source directory, this will be the ``share`` folder in the
94	   same directory.
95	
96	.. _addr_gen_example:
97	
98	The addr_gen module
99	^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
100	
101	Since we're just getting started, let's instead begin with :yoscrypt:`hierarchy
102	-top addr_gen`.  This command declares that the top level module is
103	``addr_gen``, and everything else can be discarded.
104	
105	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.v
106	   :language: Verilog
107	   :start-at: module addr_gen
108	   :end-at: endmodule //addr_gen
109	   :lineno-match:
110	   :caption: ``addr_gen`` module source
111	   :name: addr_gen-v
112	
113	.. note::
114	
115	   `hierarchy` should always be the first command after the design has been
116	   read.  By specifying the top module, `hierarchy` will also set the ``(* top
117	   *)`` attribute on it.  This is used by other commands that need to know which
118	   module is the top.
119	
120	.. use doscon for a console-like display that supports the `yosys> [command]` format.
121	
122	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
123	   :language: doscon
124	   :start-at: yosys> hierarchy -top addr_gen
125	   :end-before: yosys> select
126	   :caption: :yoscrypt:`hierarchy -top addr_gen` output
127	   :name: hierarchy_output
128	
129	Our ``addr_gen`` circuit now looks like this:
130	
131	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/addr_gen_hier.*
132	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
133	   :name: addr_gen_hier
134	
135	   ``addr_gen`` module after `hierarchy`
136	
137	Simple operations like ``addr + 1`` and ``addr == MAX_DATA-1`` can be extracted
138	from our ``always @`` block in :ref:`addr_gen-v`. This gives us the highlighted
139	`$add` and `$eq` cells we see. But control logic (like the ``if .. else``) and
140	memory elements (like the ``addr <= 0``) are not so straightforward.  These get
141	put into "processes", shown in the schematic as ``PROC``.  Note how the second
142	line refers to the line numbers of the start/end of the corresponding ``always
143	@`` block.  In the case of an ``initial`` block, we instead see the ``PROC``
144	referring to line 0.
145	
146	To handle these, let us now introduce the next command: :cmd:title:`proc`.
147	`proc` is a macro command like `synth_ice40`.  Rather than modifying the design
148	directly, it instead calls a series of other commands.  In the case of `proc`,
149	these sub-commands work to convert the behavioral logic of processes into
150	multiplexers and registers.  Let's see what happens when we run it.  For now, we
151	will call :yoscrypt:`proc -noopt` to prevent some automatic optimizations which
152	would normally happen.
153	
154	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/addr_gen_proc.*
155	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
156	   :name: addr_gen_proc
157	
158	   ``addr_gen`` module after :yoscrypt:`proc -noopt`
159	
160	There are now a few new cells from our ``always @``, which have been
161	highlighted.  The ``if`` statements are now modeled with `$mux` cells, while the
162	register uses an `$adff` cell.  If we look at the terminal output we can also
163	see all of the different ``proc_*`` commands being called.  We will look at each
164	of these in more detail in :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/proc`.
165	
166	Notice how in the top left of :ref:`addr_gen_proc` we have a floating wire,
167	generated from the initial assignment of 0 to the ``addr`` wire.  However, this
168	initial assignment is not synthesizable, so this will need to be cleaned up
169	before we can generate the physical hardware.  We can do this now by calling
170	`clean`.  We're also going to call `opt_expr` now, which would normally be
171	called at the end of `proc`.  We can call both commands at the same time by
172	separating them with a colon and space: :yoscrypt:`opt_expr; clean`.
173	
174	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/addr_gen_clean.*
175	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
176	   :name: addr_gen_clean
177	
178	   ``addr_gen`` module after :yoscrypt:`opt_expr; clean`
179	
180	You may also notice that the highlighted `$eq` cell input of ``255`` has changed
181	to ``8'11111111``.  Constant values are presented in the format
182	``<bit_width>'<bits>``, with 32-bit values instead using the decimal number.
183	This indicates that the constant input has been reduced from 32-bit wide to
184	8-bit wide.  This is a side-effect of running `opt_expr`, which performs
185	constant folding and simple expression rewriting.    For more on why this
186	happens, refer to :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt` and the :ref:`section on
187	opt_expr <adv_opt_expr>`.
188	
189	.. note::
190	
191	   :cmd:title:`clean` can also be called with two semicolons after any command,
192	   for example we could have called :yoscrypt:`opt_expr;;` instead of
193	   :yoscrypt:`opt_expr; clean`.  You may notice some scripts will end each line
194	   with ``;;``.  It is beneficial to run `clean` before inspecting intermediate
195	   products to remove disconnected parts of the circuit which have been left
196	   over, and in some cases can reduce the processing required in subsequent
197	   commands.
198	
199	.. todo:: consider a brief glossary for terms like adff
200	
201	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
202	
203	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/proc`
204	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`
205	
206	The full example
207	^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
208	
209	Let's now go back and check on our full design by using :yoscrypt:`hierarchy
210	-check -top fifo`.  By passing the ``-check`` option there we are also telling
211	the `hierarchy` command that if the design includes any non-blackbox modules
212	without an implementation it should return an error.
213	
214	Note that if we tried to run this command now then we would get an error.  This
215	is because we already removed all of the modules other than ``addr_gen``.  We
216	could restart our shell session, but instead let's use two new commands:
217	
218	- :cmd:title:`design`, and
219	- :cmd:title:`read_verilog`.
220	
221	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
222	   :language: doscon
223	   :start-at: design -reset
224	   :end-before: yosys> proc
225	   :caption: reloading :file:`fifo.v` and running :yoscrypt:`hierarchy -check -top fifo`
226	
227	Notice how this time we didn't see any of those ``$abstract`` modules?  That's
228	because when we ran ``yosys fifo.v``, the first command Yosys called was
229	:yoscrypt:`read_verilog -defer fifo.v`.  The ``-defer`` option there tells
230	`read_verilog` only read the abstract syntax tree and defer actual compilation
231	to a later `hierarchy` command. This is useful in cases where the default
232	parameters of modules yield invalid code which is not synthesizable. This is why
233	Yosys defers compilation automatically and is one of the reasons why hierarchy
234	should always be the first command after loading the design.  If we know that
235	our design won't run into this issue, we can skip the ``-defer``.
236	
237	.. todo:: `hierarchy` failure modes
238	
239	.. note::
240	
241	   The number before a command's output increments with each command run.  Don't
242	   worry if your numbers don't match ours!  The output you are seeing comes from
243	   the same script that was used to generate the images in this document,
244	   included in the source as :file:`fifo.ys`. There are extra commands being run
245	   which you don't see, but feel free to try them yourself, or play around with
246	   different commands.  You can always start over with a clean slate by calling
247	   ``exit`` or hitting :kbd:`ctrl+d` (i.e. EOF) and re-launching the Yosys
248	   interactive terminal.  :kbd:`ctrl+c` (i.e. SIGINT) will also end the terminal
249	   session but will return an error code rather than exiting gracefully.
250	
251	We can also run `proc` now to finish off the full :ref:`synth_begin`. Because
252	the design schematic is quite large, we will be showing just the data path for
253	the ``rdata`` output.  If you would like to see the entire design for yourself,
254	you can do so with :cmd:title:`show`.  Note that the `show` command only works
255	with a single module, so you may need to call it with :yoscrypt:`show fifo`.
256	:ref:`show_intro` section in :doc:`/getting_started/scripting_intro` has more on
257	how to use `show`.
258	
259	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_proc.*
260	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
261	   :name: rdata_proc
262	
263	   ``rdata`` output after `proc`
264	
265	The highlighted ``fifo_reader`` block contains an instance of the
266	:ref:`addr_gen_proc` that we looked at earlier.  Notice how the type is shown as
267	``$paramod\\addr_gen\\MAX_DATA=s32'...``.  This is a "parametric module": an
268	instance of the ``addr_gen`` module with the ``MAX_DATA`` parameter set to the
269	given value.
270	
271	The other highlighted block is a `$memrd` cell.  At this stage of synthesis we
272	don't yet know what type of memory is going to be implemented, but we *do* know
273	that ``rdata <= data[raddr];`` could be implemented as a read from memory. Note
274	that the `$memrd` cell here is asynchronous, with both the clock and enable
275	signal undefined; shown with the ``1'x`` inputs.
276	
277	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
278	   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/proc`
279	
280	Flattening
281	~~~~~~~~~~
282	
283	At this stage of a synthesis flow there are a few other commands we could run.
284	In `synth_ice40` we get these:
285	
286	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
287	   :language: yoscrypt
288	   :start-after: flatten:
289	   :end-before: coarse:
290	   :dedent:
291	   :name: synth_flatten
292	   :caption: ``flatten`` section
293	
294	We start by runnning `check`.  This doesn't affect the design, but it can
295	identify a few obvious problems which will cause errors later.  Calling it here
296	lets us fail faster rather than wasting time on something we know is impossible.
297	Next up is `flatten`.  Flattening the design like this can allow for
298	optimizations between modules which would otherwise be missed.  Let's run
299	:yoscrypt:`flatten;;` on our design.
300	
301	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
302	   :language: doscon
303	   :start-at: yosys> flatten
304	   :end-before: yosys> select
305	   :name: flat_clean
306	   :caption: output of :yoscrypt:`flatten;;`
307	
308	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_flat.*
309	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
310	   :name: rdata_flat
311	
312	   ``rdata`` output after :yoscrypt:`flatten;;`
313	
314	.. role:: yoterm(code)
315	   :language: doscon
316	
317	The pieces have moved around a bit, but we can see :ref:`addr_gen_proc` from
318	earlier has replaced the ``fifo_reader`` block in :ref:`rdata_proc`.  We can
319	also see that the ``addr`` output has been renamed to :file:`fifo_reader.addr`
320	and merged with the ``raddr`` wire feeding into the `$memrd` cell.  This wire
321	merging happened during the call to `clean` which we can see in the
322	:ref:`flat_clean`.
323	
324	.. note::
325	
326	   `flatten` and `clean` would normally be combined into a
327	   single :yoterm:`yosys> flatten;;` output, but they appear separately here as
328	   a side effect of using `echo` for generating the terminal style
329	   output.
330	
331	Depending on the target architecture, this stage of synthesis might also see
332	commands such as `tribuf` with the ``-logic`` option and `deminout`.  These
333	remove tristate and inout constructs respectively, replacing them with logic
334	suitable for mapping to an FPGA.  Since we do not have any such constructs in
335	our example running these commands does not change our design.
336	
337	The coarse-grain representation
338	~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
339	
340	At this stage, the design is in coarse-grain representation.  It still looks
341	recognizable, and cells are word-level operators with parametrizable width. This
342	is the stage of synthesis where we do things like const propagation, expression
343	rewriting, and trimming unused parts of wires.
344	
345	This is also where we convert our FSMs and hard blocks like DSPs or memories.
346	Such elements have to be inferred from patterns in the design and there are
347	special passes for each.  Detection of these patterns can also be affected by
348	optimizations and other transformations done previously.
349	
350	.. note::
351	
352	   While the iCE40 flow had a :ref:`synth_flatten` and put `proc` in the
353	   :ref:`synth_begin`, some synthesis scripts will instead include these in this
354	   section.
355	
356	Part 1
357	^^^^^^
358	
359	In the iCE40 flow, we start with the following commands:
360	
361	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
362	   :language: yoscrypt
363	   :start-after: coarse:
364	   :end-before: wreduce
365	   :dedent:
366	   :caption: ``coarse`` section (part 1)
367	   :name: synth_coarse1
368	
369	We've already come across `opt_expr` and `check`, and `opt_clean` is the same as
370	`clean` but with more verbose output.  Next up is :yoscrypt:`opt -nodffe
371	-nosdff` performing a set of simple optimizations on the design.  This command
372	also ensures that only a specific subset of FF types are included, in
373	preparation for the next command: :cmd:title:`fsm`.  Both `opt` and `fsm` are
374	macro commands which are explored in more detail in
375	:doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt` and :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/fsm`
376	respectively.
377	
378	Up until now, the data path for ``rdata`` has remained the same since
379	:ref:`rdata_flat`.  However the next call to `opt` does cause a change.
380	Specifically, the call to `opt_dff` without the ``-nodffe -nosdff`` options is
381	able to fold one of the `$mux` cells into the `$adff` to form an `$adffe` cell;
382	highlighted below:
383	
384	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
385	   :language: doscon
386	   :start-at: yosys> opt_dff
387	   :end-before: yosys> select
388	   :caption: output of `opt_dff`
389	
390	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_adffe.*
391	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
392	   :name: rdata_adffe
393	
394	   ``rdata`` output after `opt_dff`
395	
396	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
397	
398	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/fsm`
399	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`
400	
401	Part 2
402	^^^^^^
403	
404	The next group of commands performs a series of optimizations:
405	
406	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
407	   :language: yoscrypt
408	   :start-at: wreduce
409	   :end-before: t:$mul
410	   :dedent:
411	   :caption: ``coarse`` section (part 2)
412	   :name: synth_coarse2
413	
414	First up is :cmd:title:`wreduce`.  If we run this we get the following:
415	
416	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
417	   :language: doscon
418	   :start-at: yosys> wreduce
419	   :end-before: yosys> select
420	   :caption: output of `wreduce`
421	
422	Looking at the data path for ``rdata``, the most relevant of these width
423	reductions are the ones affecting ``fifo.$flatten\fifo_reader.$add$fifo.v``.
424	That is the `$add` cell incrementing the fifo_reader address.  We can look at
425	the schematic and see the output of that cell has now changed.
426	
427	.. todo:: pending bugfix in `wreduce` and/or `opt_clean`
428	
429	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_wreduce.*
430	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
431	   :name: rdata_wreduce
432	
433	   ``rdata`` output after `wreduce`
434	
435	The next two (new) commands are :cmd:title:`peepopt` and :cmd:title:`share`.
436	Neither of these affect our design, and they're explored in more detail in
437	:doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`, so let's skip over them.  :yoscrypt:`techmap
438	-map +/cmp2lut.v -D LUT_WIDTH=4` optimizes certain comparison operators by
439	converting them to LUTs instead.  The usage of `techmap` is explored more in
440	:doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`.
441	
442	Our next command to run is
443	:cmd:title:`memory_dff`.
444	
445	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
446	   :language: doscon
447	   :start-at: yosys> memory_dff
448	   :end-before: yosys> select
449	   :caption: output of `memory_dff`
450	
451	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_memrdv2.*
452	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
453	   :name: rdata_memrdv2
454	
455	   ``rdata`` output after `memory_dff`
456	
457	As the title suggests, `memory_dff` has merged the output `$dff` into the
458	`$memrd` cell and converted it to a `$memrd_v2` (highlighted).  This has also
459	connected the ``CLK`` port to the ``clk`` input as it is now a synchronous
460	memory read with appropriate enable (``EN=1'1``) and reset (``ARST=1'0`` and
461	``SRST=1'0``) inputs.
462	
463	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
464	
465	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`
466	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`
467	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/memory`
468	
469	Part 3
470	^^^^^^
471	
472	The third part of the `synth_ice40` flow is a series of commands for mapping to
473	DSPs.  By default, the iCE40 flow will not map to the hardware DSP blocks and
474	will only be performed if called with the ``-dsp`` flag: :yoscrypt:`synth_ice40
475	-dsp`.  While our example has nothing that could be mapped to DSPs we can still
476	take a quick look at the commands here and describe what they do.
477	
478	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
479	   :language: yoscrypt
480	   :start-at: t:$mul
481	   :end-before: alumacc
482	   :dedent:
483	   :caption: ``coarse`` section (part 3)
484	   :name: synth_coarse3
485	
486	:yoscrypt:`wreduce t:$mul` performs width reduction again, this time targetting
487	only cells of type `$mul`.  :yoscrypt:`techmap -map +/mul2dsp.v -map
488	+/ice40/dsp_map.v ... -D DSP_NAME=$__MUL16X16` uses `techmap` to map `$mul`
489	cells to ``$__MUL16X16`` which are, in turn, mapped to the iCE40 ``SB_MAC16``.
490	Any multipliers which aren't compatible with conversion to ``$__MUL16X16`` are
491	relabelled to ``$__soft_mul`` before `chtype` changes them back to `$mul`.
492	
493	During the mul2dsp conversion, some of the intermediate signals are marked with
494	the attribute ``mul2dsp``.  By calling :yoscrypt:`select a:mul2dsp` we restrict
495	the following commands to only operate on the cells and wires used for these
496	signals.  `setattr` removes the now unnecessary ``mul2dsp`` attribute.
497	`opt_expr` we've already come across for const folding and simple expression
498	rewriting, the ``-fine`` option just enables more fine-grain optimizations.
499	Then we perform width reduction a final time and clear the selection.
500	
501	.. todo:: ``ice40_dsp`` is pmgen
502	
503	Finally we have `ice40_dsp`: similar to the `memory_dff` command we saw in the
504	previous section, this merges any surrounding registers into the ``SB_MAC16``
505	cell.  This includes not just the input/output registers, but also pipeline
506	registers and even a post-adder where applicable: turning a multiply + add into
507	a single multiply-accumulate.
508	
509	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
510	   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`
511	
512	Part 4
513	^^^^^^
514	
515	That brings us to the fourth and final part for the iCE40 synthesis flow:
516	
517	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
518	   :language: yoscrypt
519	   :start-at: alumacc
520	   :end-before: map_ram:
521	   :dedent:
522	   :caption: ``coarse`` section (part 4)
523	   :name: synth_coarse4
524	
525	Where before each type of arithmetic operation had its own cell, e.g. `$add`, we
526	now want to extract these into `$alu` and `$macc_v2` cells which can help identify
527	opportunities for reusing logic.  We do this by running `alumacc`, which we can
528	see produce the following changes in our example design:
529	
530	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
531	   :language: doscon
532	   :start-at: yosys> alumacc
533	   :end-before: yosys> select
534	   :caption: output of `alumacc`
535	
536	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_alumacc.*
537	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
538	   :name: rdata_alumacc
539	
540	   ``rdata`` output after `alumacc`
541	
542	Once these cells have been inserted, the call to `opt` can combine cells which
543	are now identical but may have been missed due to e.g. the difference between
544	`$add` and `$sub`.
545	
546	The other new command in this part is :cmd:title:`memory`.  `memory` is another
547	macro command which we examine in more detail in
548	:doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/memory`.  For this document, let us focus just on
549	the step most relevant to our example: `memory_collect`. Up until this point,
550	our memory reads and our memory writes have been totally disjoint cells;
551	operating on the same memory only in the abstract. `memory_collect` combines all
552	of the reads and writes for a memory block into a single cell.
553	
554	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_coarse.*
555	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
556	   :name: rdata_coarse
557	
558	   ``rdata`` output after `memory_collect`
559	
560	Looking at the schematic after running `memory_collect` we see that our
561	`$memrd_v2` cell has been replaced with a `$mem_v2` cell named ``data``, the
562	same name that we used in :ref:`fifo-v`. Where before we had a single set of
563	signals for address and enable, we now have one set for reading (``RD_*``) and
564	one for writing (``WR_*``), as well as both ``WR_DATA`` input and ``RD_DATA``
565	output.
566	
567	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
568	
569	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`
570	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/memory`
571	
572	Final note
573	^^^^^^^^^^
574	
575	Having now reached the end of the the coarse-grain representation, we could also
576	have gotten here by running :yoscrypt:`synth_ice40 -top fifo -run :map_ram`
577	after loading the design.  The :yoscrypt:`-run <from_label>:<to_label>` option
578	with an empty ``<from_label>`` starts from the :ref:`synth_begin`, while the
579	``<to_label>`` runs up to but including the :ref:`map_ram`.
580	
581	Hardware mapping
582	~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
583	
584	The remaining sections each map a different type of hardware and are much more
585	architecture dependent than the previous sections.  As such we will only be
586	looking at each section very briefly.
587	
588	If you skipped calling :yoscrypt:`read_verilog -D ICE40_HX -lib -specify
589	+/ice40/cells_sim.v` earlier, do it now.
590	
591	Memory blocks
592	^^^^^^^^^^^^^
593	
594	Mapping to hard memory blocks uses a combination of `memory_libmap` and
595	`techmap`.
596	
597	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
598	   :language: yoscrypt
599	   :start-after: map_ram:
600	   :end-before: map_ffram:
601	   :dedent:
602	   :name: map_ram
603	   :caption: ``map_ram`` section
604	
605	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_ram.*
606	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
607	   :name: rdata_map_ram
608	
609	   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_ram`
610	
611	The :ref:`map_ram` converts the generic `$mem_v2` into the iCE40 ``SB_RAM40_4K``
612	(highlighted). We can also see the memory address has been remapped, and the
613	data bits have been reordered (or swizzled).  There is also now a `$mux` cell
614	controlling the value of ``rdata``.  In :ref:`fifo-v` we wrote our memory as
615	read-before-write, however the ``SB_RAM40_4K`` has undefined behaviour when
616	reading from and writing to the same address in the same cycle. As a result,
617	extra logic is added so that the generated circuit matches the behaviour of the
618	verilog.  :ref:`no_rw_check` describes how we could change our verilog to match
619	our hardware instead.
620	
621	If we run `memory_libmap` under the `debug` command we can see candidates which
622	were identified for mapping, along with the costs of each and what logic
623	requires emulation.
624	
625	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.libmap
626	   :language: doscon
627	   :lines: 2, 6-
628	
629	The ``$__ICE40_RAM4K_`` cell is defined in the file |techlibs/ice40/brams.txt|_,
630	with the mapping to ``SB_RAM40_4K`` done by `techmap` using
631	|techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v|_.  Any leftover memory cells are then converted
632	into flip flops (the ``logic fallback``) with `memory_map`.
633	
634	.. |techlibs/ice40/brams.txt| replace:: :file:`techlibs/ice40/brams.txt`
635	.. _techlibs/ice40/brams.txt: https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys/tree/main/techlibs/ice40/brams.txt
636	.. |techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v| replace:: :file:`techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v`
637	.. _techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v: https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys/tree/main/techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v
638	
639	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
640	   :language: yoscrypt
641	   :start-after: map_ffram:
642	   :end-before: map_gates:
643	   :dedent:
644	   :name: map_ffram
645	   :caption: ``map_ffram`` section
646	
647	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_ffram.*
648	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
649	   :name: rdata_map_ffram
650	
651	   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_ffram`
652	
653	.. note::
654	
655	   The visual clutter on the ``RDATA`` output port (highlighted) is an
656	   unfortunate side effect of `opt_clean` on the swizzled data bits. In
657	   connecting the `$mux` input port directly to ``RDATA`` to reduce the number
658	   of wires, the ``$techmap579\data.0.0.RDATA`` wire becomes more visually
659	   complex.
660	
661	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
662	
663	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`
664	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/memory`
665	
666	Arithmetic
667	^^^^^^^^^^
668	
669	Uses `techmap` to map basic arithmetic logic to hardware.  This sees somewhat of
670	an explosion in cells as multi-bit `$mux` and `$adffe` are replaced with
671	single-bit `$_MUX_` and `$_DFFE_PP0P_` cells, while the `$alu` is replaced with
672	primitive `$_OR_` and `$_NOT_` gates and a `$lut` cell.
673	
674	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
675	   :language: yoscrypt
676	   :start-after: map_gates:
677	   :end-before: map_ffs:
678	   :dedent:
679	   :name: map_gates
680	   :caption: ``map_gates`` section
681	
682	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_gates.*
683	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
684	   :name: rdata_map_gates
685	
686	   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_gates`
687	
688	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
689	   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`
690	
691	Flip-flops
692	^^^^^^^^^^
693	
694	Convert FFs to the types supported in hardware with `dfflegalize`, and then use
695	`techmap` to map them.  In our example, this converts the `$_DFFE_PP0P_` cells
696	to ``SB_DFFER``.
697	
698	We also run `simplemap` here to convert any remaining cells which could not be
699	mapped to hardware into gate-level primitives.  This includes optimizing
700	`$_MUX_` cells where one of the inputs is a constant ``1'0``, replacing it
701	instead with an `$_AND_` cell.
702	
703	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
704	   :language: yoscrypt
705	   :start-after: map_ffs:
706	   :end-before: map_luts:
707	   :dedent:
708	   :name: map_ffs
709	   :caption: ``map_ffs`` section
710	
711	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_ffs.*
712	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
713	   :name: rdata_map_ffs
714	
715	   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_ffs`
716	
717	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
718	   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`
719	
720	LUTs
721	^^^^
722	
723	`abc` and `techmap` are used to map LUTs; converting primitive cell types to use
724	`$lut` and ``SB_CARRY`` cells.  Note that the iCE40 flow uses `abc9` rather than
725	`abc`. For more on what these do, and what the difference between these two
726	commands are, refer to :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/abc`.
727	
728	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
729	   :language: yoscrypt
730	   :start-after: map_luts:
731	   :end-before: map_cells:
732	   :dedent:
733	   :name: map_luts
734	   :caption: ``map_luts`` section
735	
736	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_luts.*
737	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
738	   :name: rdata_map_luts
739	
740	   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_luts`
741	
742	Finally we use `techmap` to map the generic `$lut` cells to iCE40 ``SB_LUT4``
743	cells.
744	
745	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
746	   :language: yoscrypt
747	   :start-after: map_cells:
748	   :end-before: check:
749	   :dedent:
750	   :name: map_cells
751	   :caption: ``map_cells`` section
752	
753	.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_cells.*
754	   :class: width-helper invert-helper
755	   :name: rdata_map_cells
756	
757	   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_cells`
758	
759	.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
760	
761	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`
762	   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/abc`
763	
764	Other cells
765	^^^^^^^^^^^
766	
767	The following commands may also be used for mapping other cells:
768	
769	`hilomap`
770	    Some architectures require special driver cells for driving a constant hi or
771	    lo value. This command replaces simple constants with instances of such
772	    driver cells.
773	
774	`iopadmap`
775	    Top-level input/outputs must usually be implemented using special I/O-pad
776	    cells. This command inserts such cells to the design.
777	
778	These commands tend to either be in the :ref:`map_cells` or after the
779	:ref:`check` depending on the flow.
780	
781	Final steps
782	~~~~~~~~~~~~
783	
784	The next section of the iCE40 synth flow performs some sanity checking and final
785	tidy up:
786	
787	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
788	   :language: yoscrypt
789	   :start-after: check:
790	   :dedent:
791	   :name: check
792	   :caption: ``check`` section
793	
794	The new commands here are:
795	
796	- :cmd:title:`autoname`,
797	- :cmd:title:`stat`, and
798	- :cmd:title:`blackbox`.
799	
800	The output from `stat` is useful for checking resource utilization; providing a
801	list of cells used in the design and the number of each, as well as the number
802	of other resources used such as wires and processes.  For this design, the final
803	call to `stat` should look something like the following:
804	
805	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.stat
806	   :language: doscon
807	   :start-at: yosys> stat -top fifo
808	
809	Note that the :yoscrypt:`-top fifo` here is optional.  `stat` will automatically
810	use the module with the ``top`` attribute set, which ``fifo`` was when we called
811	`hierarchy`.  If no module is marked ``top``, then stats will be shown for each
812	module selected.
813	
814	The `stat` output is also useful as a kind of sanity-check: Since we have
815	already run `proc`, we wouldn't expect there to be any processes. We also expect
816	``data`` to use hard memory; if instead of an ``SB_RAM40_4K`` saw a high number
817	of flip-flops being used we might suspect something was wrong.
818	
819	If we instead called `stat` immediately after :yoscrypt:`read_verilog fifo.v` we
820	would see something very different:
821	
822	.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.stat
823	   :language: doscon
824	   :start-at: yosys> stat
825	   :end-before: yosys> stat -top fifo
826	
827	Notice how ``fifo`` and ``addr_gen`` are listed separately, and the statistics
828	for ``fifo`` show 2 ``addr_gen`` modules.  Because this is before the memory has
829	been mapped, we also see that there is 1 memory with 2048 memory bits; matching
830	our 8-bit wide ``data`` memory with 256 values (:math:`8*256=2048`).
831	
832	Synthesis output
833	^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
834	
835	The iCE40 synthesis flow has the following output modes available:
836	
837	- `write_blif`,
838	- `write_edif`, and
839	- `write_json`.
840	
841	As an example, if we called :yoscrypt:`synth_ice40 -top fifo -json fifo.json`,
842	our synthesized ``fifo`` design will be output as :file:`fifo.json`.  We can
843	then read the design back into Yosys with `read_json`, but make sure you use
844	:yoscrypt:`design -reset` or open a new interactive terminal first.  The JSON
845	output we get can also be loaded into `nextpnr`_ to do place and route; but that
846	is beyond the scope of this documentation.
847	
848	.. _nextpnr: https://github.com/YosysHQ/nextpnr
849	
850	.. seealso:: :cmd:title:`synth_ice40`
851

file.content:
Synthesis starter
-----------------

This page will be a guided walkthrough of the prepackaged iCE40 FPGA synthesis
script - `synth_ice40`.  We will take a simple design through each step, looking
at the commands being called and what they do to the design. While `synth_ice40`
is specific to the iCE40 platform, most of the operations we will be discussing
are common across the majority of FPGA synthesis scripts. Thus, this document
will provide a good foundational understanding of how synthesis in Yosys is
performed, regardless of the actual architecture being used.

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/synth`

Demo design
~~~~~~~~~~~

.. role:: yoscrypt(code)
   :language: yoscrypt

First, let's quickly look at the design we'll be synthesizing:

.. todo:: reconsider including the whole (~77 line) design like this

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.v
   :language: Verilog
   :linenos:
   :caption: :file:`fifo.v`
   :name: fifo-v

.. todo:: fifo.v description

While the open source `read_verilog` frontend generally does a pretty good job
at processing valid Verilog input, it does not provide very good error handling
or reporting.  Using an external tool such as `verilator`_ before running Yosys
is highly recommended. We can quickly check the Verilog syntax of our design by
calling ``verilator --lint-only fifo.v``.

.. _verilator: https://www.veripool.org/verilator/

Loading the design
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Let's load the design into Yosys.  From the command line, we can call ``yosys
fifo.v``.  This will open an interactive Yosys shell session and immediately
parse the code from :ref:`fifo-v` and convert it into an Abstract Syntax Tree
(AST).  If you are interested in how this happens, there is more information in
the document, :doc:`/yosys_internals/flow/verilog_frontend`.  For now, suffice
it to say that we do this to simplify further processing of the design.  You
should see something like the following:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
   :language: console
   :start-at: $ yosys fifo.v
   :end-before: echo on

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
   :doc:`/using_yosys/more_scripting/load_design`

Elaboration
~~~~~~~~~~~

Now that we are in the interactive shell, we can call Yosys commands directly.
Our overall goal is to call :yoscrypt:`synth_ice40 -top fifo`, but for now we
can run each of the commands individually for a better sense of how each part
contributes to the flow.  We will also start with just a single module;
``addr_gen``.

At the bottom of the `help` output for
`synth_ice40` is the complete list of commands called by this script.
Let's start with the section labeled ``begin``:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: begin:
   :end-before: flatten:
   :dedent:
   :caption: ``begin`` section
   :name: synth_begin

:yoscrypt:`read_verilog -D ICE40_HX -lib -specify +/ice40/cells_sim.v` loads the
iCE40 cell models which allows us to include platform specific IP blocks in our
design.  PLLs are a common example of this, where we might need to reference
``SB_PLL40_CORE`` directly rather than being able to rely on mapping passes
later.  Since our simple design doesn't use any of these IP blocks, we can skip
this command for now.  Because these cell models will also be needed once we
start mapping to hardware we will still need to load them later.

.. note::

   ``+/`` is a dynamic reference to the Yosys ``share`` directory.  By default,
   this is ``/usr/local/share/yosys``.  If using a locally built version of
   Yosys from the source directory, this will be the ``share`` folder in the
   same directory.

.. _addr_gen_example:

The addr_gen module
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Since we're just getting started, let's instead begin with :yoscrypt:`hierarchy
-top addr_gen`.  This command declares that the top level module is
``addr_gen``, and everything else can be discarded.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.v
   :language: Verilog
   :start-at: module addr_gen
   :end-at: endmodule //addr_gen
   :lineno-match:
   :caption: ``addr_gen`` module source
   :name: addr_gen-v

.. note::

   `hierarchy` should always be the first command after the design has been
   read.  By specifying the top module, `hierarchy` will also set the ``(* top
   *)`` attribute on it.  This is used by other commands that need to know which
   module is the top.

.. use doscon for a console-like display that supports the `yosys> [command]` format.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
   :language: doscon
   :start-at: yosys> hierarchy -top addr_gen
   :end-before: yosys> select
   :caption: :yoscrypt:`hierarchy -top addr_gen` output
   :name: hierarchy_output

Our ``addr_gen`` circuit now looks like this:

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/addr_gen_hier.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: addr_gen_hier

   ``addr_gen`` module after `hierarchy`

Simple operations like ``addr + 1`` and ``addr == MAX_DATA-1`` can be extracted
from our ``always @`` block in :ref:`addr_gen-v`. This gives us the highlighted
`$add` and `$eq` cells we see. But control logic (like the ``if .. else``) and
memory elements (like the ``addr <= 0``) are not so straightforward.  These get
put into "processes", shown in the schematic as ``PROC``.  Note how the second
line refers to the line numbers of the start/end of the corresponding ``always
@`` block.  In the case of an ``initial`` block, we instead see the ``PROC``
referring to line 0.

To handle these, let us now introduce the next command: :cmd:title:`proc`.
`proc` is a macro command like `synth_ice40`.  Rather than modifying the design
directly, it instead calls a series of other commands.  In the case of `proc`,
these sub-commands work to convert the behavioral logic of processes into
multiplexers and registers.  Let's see what happens when we run it.  For now, we
will call :yoscrypt:`proc -noopt` to prevent some automatic optimizations which
would normally happen.

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/addr_gen_proc.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: addr_gen_proc

   ``addr_gen`` module after :yoscrypt:`proc -noopt`

There are now a few new cells from our ``always @``, which have been
highlighted.  The ``if`` statements are now modeled with `$mux` cells, while the
register uses an `$adff` cell.  If we look at the terminal output we can also
see all of the different ``proc_*`` commands being called.  We will look at each
of these in more detail in :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/proc`.

Notice how in the top left of :ref:`addr_gen_proc` we have a floating wire,
generated from the initial assignment of 0 to the ``addr`` wire.  However, this
initial assignment is not synthesizable, so this will need to be cleaned up
before we can generate the physical hardware.  We can do this now by calling
`clean`.  We're also going to call `opt_expr` now, which would normally be
called at the end of `proc`.  We can call both commands at the same time by
separating them with a colon and space: :yoscrypt:`opt_expr; clean`.

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/addr_gen_clean.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: addr_gen_clean

   ``addr_gen`` module after :yoscrypt:`opt_expr; clean`

You may also notice that the highlighted `$eq` cell input of ``255`` has changed
to ``8'11111111``.  Constant values are presented in the format
``<bit_width>'<bits>``, with 32-bit values instead using the decimal number.
This indicates that the constant input has been reduced from 32-bit wide to
8-bit wide.  This is a side-effect of running `opt_expr`, which performs
constant folding and simple expression rewriting.    For more on why this
happens, refer to :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt` and the :ref:`section on
opt_expr <adv_opt_expr>`.

.. note::

   :cmd:title:`clean` can also be called with two semicolons after any command,
   for example we could have called :yoscrypt:`opt_expr;;` instead of
   :yoscrypt:`opt_expr; clean`.  You may notice some scripts will end each line
   with ``;;``.  It is beneficial to run `clean` before inspecting intermediate
   products to remove disconnected parts of the circuit which have been left
   over, and in some cases can reduce the processing required in subsequent
   commands.

.. todo:: consider a brief glossary for terms like adff

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for

   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/proc`
   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`

The full example
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Let's now go back and check on our full design by using :yoscrypt:`hierarchy
-check -top fifo`.  By passing the ``-check`` option there we are also telling
the `hierarchy` command that if the design includes any non-blackbox modules
without an implementation it should return an error.

Note that if we tried to run this command now then we would get an error.  This
is because we already removed all of the modules other than ``addr_gen``.  We
could restart our shell session, but instead let's use two new commands:

- :cmd:title:`design`, and
- :cmd:title:`read_verilog`.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
   :language: doscon
   :start-at: design -reset
   :end-before: yosys> proc
   :caption: reloading :file:`fifo.v` and running :yoscrypt:`hierarchy -check -top fifo`

Notice how this time we didn't see any of those ``$abstract`` modules?  That's
because when we ran ``yosys fifo.v``, the first command Yosys called was
:yoscrypt:`read_verilog -defer fifo.v`.  The ``-defer`` option there tells
`read_verilog` only read the abstract syntax tree and defer actual compilation
to a later `hierarchy` command. This is useful in cases where the default
parameters of modules yield invalid code which is not synthesizable. This is why
Yosys defers compilation automatically and is one of the reasons why hierarchy
should always be the first command after loading the design.  If we know that
our design won't run into this issue, we can skip the ``-defer``.

.. todo:: `hierarchy` failure modes

.. note::

   The number before a command's output increments with each command run.  Don't
   worry if your numbers don't match ours!  The output you are seeing comes from
   the same script that was used to generate the images in this document,
   included in the source as :file:`fifo.ys`. There are extra commands being run
   which you don't see, but feel free to try them yourself, or play around with
   different commands.  You can always start over with a clean slate by calling
   ``exit`` or hitting :kbd:`ctrl+d` (i.e. EOF) and re-launching the Yosys
   interactive terminal.  :kbd:`ctrl+c` (i.e. SIGINT) will also end the terminal
   session but will return an error code rather than exiting gracefully.

We can also run `proc` now to finish off the full :ref:`synth_begin`. Because
the design schematic is quite large, we will be showing just the data path for
the ``rdata`` output.  If you would like to see the entire design for yourself,
you can do so with :cmd:title:`show`.  Note that the `show` command only works
with a single module, so you may need to call it with :yoscrypt:`show fifo`.
:ref:`show_intro` section in :doc:`/getting_started/scripting_intro` has more on
how to use `show`.

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_proc.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_proc

   ``rdata`` output after `proc`

The highlighted ``fifo_reader`` block contains an instance of the
:ref:`addr_gen_proc` that we looked at earlier.  Notice how the type is shown as
``$paramod\\addr_gen\\MAX_DATA=s32'...``.  This is a "parametric module": an
instance of the ``addr_gen`` module with the ``MAX_DATA`` parameter set to the
given value.

The other highlighted block is a `$memrd` cell.  At this stage of synthesis we
don't yet know what type of memory is going to be implemented, but we *do* know
that ``rdata <= data[raddr];`` could be implemented as a read from memory. Note
that the `$memrd` cell here is asynchronous, with both the clock and enable
signal undefined; shown with the ``1'x`` inputs.

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/proc`

Flattening
~~~~~~~~~~

At this stage of a synthesis flow there are a few other commands we could run.
In `synth_ice40` we get these:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: flatten:
   :end-before: coarse:
   :dedent:
   :name: synth_flatten
   :caption: ``flatten`` section

We start by runnning `check`.  This doesn't affect the design, but it can
identify a few obvious problems which will cause errors later.  Calling it here
lets us fail faster rather than wasting time on something we know is impossible.
Next up is `flatten`.  Flattening the design like this can allow for
optimizations between modules which would otherwise be missed.  Let's run
:yoscrypt:`flatten;;` on our design.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
   :language: doscon
   :start-at: yosys> flatten
   :end-before: yosys> select
   :name: flat_clean
   :caption: output of :yoscrypt:`flatten;;`

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_flat.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_flat

   ``rdata`` output after :yoscrypt:`flatten;;`

.. role:: yoterm(code)
   :language: doscon

The pieces have moved around a bit, but we can see :ref:`addr_gen_proc` from
earlier has replaced the ``fifo_reader`` block in :ref:`rdata_proc`.  We can
also see that the ``addr`` output has been renamed to :file:`fifo_reader.addr`
and merged with the ``raddr`` wire feeding into the `$memrd` cell.  This wire
merging happened during the call to `clean` which we can see in the
:ref:`flat_clean`.

.. note::

   `flatten` and `clean` would normally be combined into a
   single :yoterm:`yosys> flatten;;` output, but they appear separately here as
   a side effect of using `echo` for generating the terminal style
   output.

Depending on the target architecture, this stage of synthesis might also see
commands such as `tribuf` with the ``-logic`` option and `deminout`.  These
remove tristate and inout constructs respectively, replacing them with logic
suitable for mapping to an FPGA.  Since we do not have any such constructs in
our example running these commands does not change our design.

The coarse-grain representation
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

At this stage, the design is in coarse-grain representation.  It still looks
recognizable, and cells are word-level operators with parametrizable width. This
is the stage of synthesis where we do things like const propagation, expression
rewriting, and trimming unused parts of wires.

This is also where we convert our FSMs and hard blocks like DSPs or memories.
Such elements have to be inferred from patterns in the design and there are
special passes for each.  Detection of these patterns can also be affected by
optimizations and other transformations done previously.

.. note::

   While the iCE40 flow had a :ref:`synth_flatten` and put `proc` in the
   :ref:`synth_begin`, some synthesis scripts will instead include these in this
   section.

Part 1
^^^^^^

In the iCE40 flow, we start with the following commands:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: coarse:
   :end-before: wreduce
   :dedent:
   :caption: ``coarse`` section (part 1)
   :name: synth_coarse1

We've already come across `opt_expr` and `check`, and `opt_clean` is the same as
`clean` but with more verbose output.  Next up is :yoscrypt:`opt -nodffe
-nosdff` performing a set of simple optimizations on the design.  This command
also ensures that only a specific subset of FF types are included, in
preparation for the next command: :cmd:title:`fsm`.  Both `opt` and `fsm` are
macro commands which are explored in more detail in
:doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt` and :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/fsm`
respectively.

Up until now, the data path for ``rdata`` has remained the same since
:ref:`rdata_flat`.  However the next call to `opt` does cause a change.
Specifically, the call to `opt_dff` without the ``-nodffe -nosdff`` options is
able to fold one of the `$mux` cells into the `$adff` to form an `$adffe` cell;
highlighted below:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
   :language: doscon
   :start-at: yosys> opt_dff
   :end-before: yosys> select
   :caption: output of `opt_dff`

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_adffe.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_adffe

   ``rdata`` output after `opt_dff`

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for

   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/fsm`
   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`

Part 2
^^^^^^

The next group of commands performs a series of optimizations:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-at: wreduce
   :end-before: t:$mul
   :dedent:
   :caption: ``coarse`` section (part 2)
   :name: synth_coarse2

First up is :cmd:title:`wreduce`.  If we run this we get the following:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
   :language: doscon
   :start-at: yosys> wreduce
   :end-before: yosys> select
   :caption: output of `wreduce`

Looking at the data path for ``rdata``, the most relevant of these width
reductions are the ones affecting ``fifo.$flatten\fifo_reader.$add$fifo.v``.
That is the `$add` cell incrementing the fifo_reader address.  We can look at
the schematic and see the output of that cell has now changed.

.. todo:: pending bugfix in `wreduce` and/or `opt_clean`

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_wreduce.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_wreduce

   ``rdata`` output after `wreduce`

The next two (new) commands are :cmd:title:`peepopt` and :cmd:title:`share`.
Neither of these affect our design, and they're explored in more detail in
:doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`, so let's skip over them.  :yoscrypt:`techmap
-map +/cmp2lut.v -D LUT_WIDTH=4` optimizes certain comparison operators by
converting them to LUTs instead.  The usage of `techmap` is explored more in
:doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`.

Our next command to run is
:cmd:title:`memory_dff`.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
   :language: doscon
   :start-at: yosys> memory_dff
   :end-before: yosys> select
   :caption: output of `memory_dff`

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_memrdv2.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_memrdv2

   ``rdata`` output after `memory_dff`

As the title suggests, `memory_dff` has merged the output `$dff` into the
`$memrd` cell and converted it to a `$memrd_v2` (highlighted).  This has also
connected the ``CLK`` port to the ``clk`` input as it is now a synchronous
memory read with appropriate enable (``EN=1'1``) and reset (``ARST=1'0`` and
``SRST=1'0``) inputs.

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for

   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`
   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`
   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/memory`

Part 3
^^^^^^

The third part of the `synth_ice40` flow is a series of commands for mapping to
DSPs.  By default, the iCE40 flow will not map to the hardware DSP blocks and
will only be performed if called with the ``-dsp`` flag: :yoscrypt:`synth_ice40
-dsp`.  While our example has nothing that could be mapped to DSPs we can still
take a quick look at the commands here and describe what they do.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-at: t:$mul
   :end-before: alumacc
   :dedent:
   :caption: ``coarse`` section (part 3)
   :name: synth_coarse3

:yoscrypt:`wreduce t:$mul` performs width reduction again, this time targetting
only cells of type `$mul`.  :yoscrypt:`techmap -map +/mul2dsp.v -map
+/ice40/dsp_map.v ... -D DSP_NAME=$__MUL16X16` uses `techmap` to map `$mul`
cells to ``$__MUL16X16`` which are, in turn, mapped to the iCE40 ``SB_MAC16``.
Any multipliers which aren't compatible with conversion to ``$__MUL16X16`` are
relabelled to ``$__soft_mul`` before `chtype` changes them back to `$mul`.

During the mul2dsp conversion, some of the intermediate signals are marked with
the attribute ``mul2dsp``.  By calling :yoscrypt:`select a:mul2dsp` we restrict
the following commands to only operate on the cells and wires used for these
signals.  `setattr` removes the now unnecessary ``mul2dsp`` attribute.
`opt_expr` we've already come across for const folding and simple expression
rewriting, the ``-fine`` option just enables more fine-grain optimizations.
Then we perform width reduction a final time and clear the selection.

.. todo:: ``ice40_dsp`` is pmgen

Finally we have `ice40_dsp`: similar to the `memory_dff` command we saw in the
previous section, this merges any surrounding registers into the ``SB_MAC16``
cell.  This includes not just the input/output registers, but also pipeline
registers and even a post-adder where applicable: turning a multiply + add into
a single multiply-accumulate.

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`

Part 4
^^^^^^

That brings us to the fourth and final part for the iCE40 synthesis flow:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-at: alumacc
   :end-before: map_ram:
   :dedent:
   :caption: ``coarse`` section (part 4)
   :name: synth_coarse4

Where before each type of arithmetic operation had its own cell, e.g. `$add`, we
now want to extract these into `$alu` and `$macc_v2` cells which can help identify
opportunities for reusing logic.  We do this by running `alumacc`, which we can
see produce the following changes in our example design:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.out
   :language: doscon
   :start-at: yosys> alumacc
   :end-before: yosys> select
   :caption: output of `alumacc`

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_alumacc.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_alumacc

   ``rdata`` output after `alumacc`

Once these cells have been inserted, the call to `opt` can combine cells which
are now identical but may have been missed due to e.g. the difference between
`$add` and `$sub`.

The other new command in this part is :cmd:title:`memory`.  `memory` is another
macro command which we examine in more detail in
:doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/memory`.  For this document, let us focus just on
the step most relevant to our example: `memory_collect`. Up until this point,
our memory reads and our memory writes have been totally disjoint cells;
operating on the same memory only in the abstract. `memory_collect` combines all
of the reads and writes for a memory block into a single cell.

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_coarse.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_coarse

   ``rdata`` output after `memory_collect`

Looking at the schematic after running `memory_collect` we see that our
`$memrd_v2` cell has been replaced with a `$mem_v2` cell named ``data``, the
same name that we used in :ref:`fifo-v`. Where before we had a single set of
signals for address and enable, we now have one set for reading (``RD_*``) and
one for writing (``WR_*``), as well as both ``WR_DATA`` input and ``RD_DATA``
output.

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for

   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/opt`
   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/memory`

Final note
^^^^^^^^^^

Having now reached the end of the the coarse-grain representation, we could also
have gotten here by running :yoscrypt:`synth_ice40 -top fifo -run :map_ram`
after loading the design.  The :yoscrypt:`-run <from_label>:<to_label>` option
with an empty ``<from_label>`` starts from the :ref:`synth_begin`, while the
``<to_label>`` runs up to but including the :ref:`map_ram`.

Hardware mapping
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

The remaining sections each map a different type of hardware and are much more
architecture dependent than the previous sections.  As such we will only be
looking at each section very briefly.

If you skipped calling :yoscrypt:`read_verilog -D ICE40_HX -lib -specify
+/ice40/cells_sim.v` earlier, do it now.

Memory blocks
^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Mapping to hard memory blocks uses a combination of `memory_libmap` and
`techmap`.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: map_ram:
   :end-before: map_ffram:
   :dedent:
   :name: map_ram
   :caption: ``map_ram`` section

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_ram.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_map_ram

   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_ram`

The :ref:`map_ram` converts the generic `$mem_v2` into the iCE40 ``SB_RAM40_4K``
(highlighted). We can also see the memory address has been remapped, and the
data bits have been reordered (or swizzled).  There is also now a `$mux` cell
controlling the value of ``rdata``.  In :ref:`fifo-v` we wrote our memory as
read-before-write, however the ``SB_RAM40_4K`` has undefined behaviour when
reading from and writing to the same address in the same cycle. As a result,
extra logic is added so that the generated circuit matches the behaviour of the
verilog.  :ref:`no_rw_check` describes how we could change our verilog to match
our hardware instead.

If we run `memory_libmap` under the `debug` command we can see candidates which
were identified for mapping, along with the costs of each and what logic
requires emulation.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.libmap
   :language: doscon
   :lines: 2, 6-

The ``$__ICE40_RAM4K_`` cell is defined in the file |techlibs/ice40/brams.txt|_,
with the mapping to ``SB_RAM40_4K`` done by `techmap` using
|techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v|_.  Any leftover memory cells are then converted
into flip flops (the ``logic fallback``) with `memory_map`.

.. |techlibs/ice40/brams.txt| replace:: :file:`techlibs/ice40/brams.txt`
.. _techlibs/ice40/brams.txt: https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys/tree/main/techlibs/ice40/brams.txt
.. |techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v| replace:: :file:`techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v`
.. _techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v: https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys/tree/main/techlibs/ice40/brams_map.v

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: map_ffram:
   :end-before: map_gates:
   :dedent:
   :name: map_ffram
   :caption: ``map_ffram`` section

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_ffram.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_map_ffram

   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_ffram`

.. note::

   The visual clutter on the ``RDATA`` output port (highlighted) is an
   unfortunate side effect of `opt_clean` on the swizzled data bits. In
   connecting the `$mux` input port directly to ``RDATA`` to reduce the number
   of wires, the ``$techmap579\data.0.0.RDATA`` wire becomes more visually
   complex.

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for

   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`
   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/memory`

Arithmetic
^^^^^^^^^^

Uses `techmap` to map basic arithmetic logic to hardware.  This sees somewhat of
an explosion in cells as multi-bit `$mux` and `$adffe` are replaced with
single-bit `$_MUX_` and `$_DFFE_PP0P_` cells, while the `$alu` is replaced with
primitive `$_OR_` and `$_NOT_` gates and a `$lut` cell.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: map_gates:
   :end-before: map_ffs:
   :dedent:
   :name: map_gates
   :caption: ``map_gates`` section

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_gates.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_map_gates

   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_gates`

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`

Flip-flops
^^^^^^^^^^

Convert FFs to the types supported in hardware with `dfflegalize`, and then use
`techmap` to map them.  In our example, this converts the `$_DFFE_PP0P_` cells
to ``SB_DFFER``.

We also run `simplemap` here to convert any remaining cells which could not be
mapped to hardware into gate-level primitives.  This includes optimizing
`$_MUX_` cells where one of the inputs is a constant ``1'0``, replacing it
instead with an `$_AND_` cell.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: map_ffs:
   :end-before: map_luts:
   :dedent:
   :name: map_ffs
   :caption: ``map_ffs`` section

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_ffs.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_map_ffs

   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_ffs`

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for
   :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`

LUTs
^^^^

`abc` and `techmap` are used to map LUTs; converting primitive cell types to use
`$lut` and ``SB_CARRY`` cells.  Note that the iCE40 flow uses `abc9` rather than
`abc`. For more on what these do, and what the difference between these two
commands are, refer to :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/abc`.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: map_luts:
   :end-before: map_cells:
   :dedent:
   :name: map_luts
   :caption: ``map_luts`` section

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_luts.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_map_luts

   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_luts`

Finally we use `techmap` to map the generic `$lut` cells to iCE40 ``SB_LUT4``
cells.

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: map_cells:
   :end-before: check:
   :dedent:
   :name: map_cells
   :caption: ``map_cells`` section

.. figure:: /_images/code_examples/fifo/rdata_map_cells.*
   :class: width-helper invert-helper
   :name: rdata_map_cells

   ``rdata`` output after :ref:`map_cells`

.. seealso:: Advanced usage docs for

   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/techmap_synth`
   - :doc:`/using_yosys/synthesis/abc`

Other cells
^^^^^^^^^^^

The following commands may also be used for mapping other cells:

`hilomap`
    Some architectures require special driver cells for driving a constant hi or
    lo value. This command replaces simple constants with instances of such
    driver cells.

`iopadmap`
    Top-level input/outputs must usually be implemented using special I/O-pad
    cells. This command inserts such cells to the design.

These commands tend to either be in the :ref:`map_cells` or after the
:ref:`check` depending on the flow.

Final steps
~~~~~~~~~~~~

The next section of the iCE40 synth flow performs some sanity checking and final
tidy up:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/macro_commands/synth_ice40.ys
   :language: yoscrypt
   :start-after: check:
   :dedent:
   :name: check
   :caption: ``check`` section

The new commands here are:

- :cmd:title:`autoname`,
- :cmd:title:`stat`, and
- :cmd:title:`blackbox`.

The output from `stat` is useful for checking resource utilization; providing a
list of cells used in the design and the number of each, as well as the number
of other resources used such as wires and processes.  For this design, the final
call to `stat` should look something like the following:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.stat
   :language: doscon
   :start-at: yosys> stat -top fifo

Note that the :yoscrypt:`-top fifo` here is optional.  `stat` will automatically
use the module with the ``top`` attribute set, which ``fifo`` was when we called
`hierarchy`.  If no module is marked ``top``, then stats will be shown for each
module selected.

The `stat` output is also useful as a kind of sanity-check: Since we have
already run `proc`, we wouldn't expect there to be any processes. We also expect
``data`` to use hard memory; if instead of an ``SB_RAM40_4K`` saw a high number
of flip-flops being used we might suspect something was wrong.

If we instead called `stat` immediately after :yoscrypt:`read_verilog fifo.v` we
would see something very different:

.. literalinclude:: /code_examples/fifo/fifo.stat
   :language: doscon
   :start-at: yosys> stat
   :end-before: yosys> stat -top fifo

Notice how ``fifo`` and ``addr_gen`` are listed separately, and the statistics
for ``fifo`` show 2 ``addr_gen`` modules.  Because this is before the memory has
been mapped, we also see that there is 1 memory with 2048 memory bits; matching
our 8-bit wide ``data`` memory with 256 values (:math:`8*256=2048`).

Synthesis output
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

The iCE40 synthesis flow has the following output modes available:

- `write_blif`,
- `write_edif`, and
- `write_json`.

As an example, if we called :yoscrypt:`synth_ice40 -top fifo -json fifo.json`,
our synthesized ``fifo`` design will be output as :file:`fifo.json`.  We can
then read the design back into Yosys with `read_json`, but make sure you use
:yoscrypt:`design -reset` or open a new interactive terminal first.  The JSON
output we get can also be loaded into `nextpnr`_ to do place and route; but that
is beyond the scope of this documentation.

.. _nextpnr: https://github.com/YosysHQ/nextpnr

.. seealso:: :cmd:title:`synth_ice40`

6. **shell()** (yosys.cc:988-1054): interactive loop using readline, prompts with create_prompt, calls `Pass::call(design, command)` per line. Handles `exit`.

7. **ShellPass** (yosys.cc:1056): registers `shell` command, explains prompts `yosys>`, `yosys*>`, `yosys [modname]>`.

8. **run_pass** (yosys.cc:857-865): logs and calls Pass::call.

9. **run_backend** (yosys.cc:867-907): auto-detects backend by extension, calls Backend::backend_call.

10. **run_frontend** (yosys.cc:723+): auto-detects frontend by extension (.ys → script).

11. **Pass::call(string)** (register.cc:210-275): tokenizes command, handles `!` shell escape, `#` comments, `;` (double `;;` → clean, triple `;;;` → clean -purge), newline termination.

12. **Pass::call(vector)** (register.cc:276-305): looks up pass_register[args[0]], calls pass->execute.

13. **scripting_intro.rst**: script format, `#` comments, `;` terminator, `!` shell escape, `-p` option, select/show intros.

14. **example_synth.rst**: guided walkthrough of synth_ice40 — great context for the synth flow stages (begin/coarse/fine/map), hierarchy, proc, opt, etc. The `stat` command explanation.

15. The README says this example generates spice output. But it's still a valid synthesis example for our purposes.

Now, there's a nuance: the manifest says the practice is "运行 examples/cmos 中的 counter.ys 脚本，用 write_verilog 输出综合后网表，再用 stat 命令统计单元数量，记录每个阶段单元数的变化。"

The counter.ys already does write_verilog synth.v and stat -liberty. So the practice aligns well.

Let me note: cmos_cells.lib is a binary detection issue with the Read tool, but `cat` works fine — it's actually a text liberty file. I'll reference it as a liberty text file.

Now, one important consideration about the `-S` flag — in u1-l2 summary it says `-S` is `synth` shortcut. Let me verify that's accurate but I don't strictly need it.

Also the `read_verilog -lib` — let me make sure I describe it correctly. The `-lib` option treats the file as a library (creates blackbox/wire declarations only). Actually `-lib` creates "library file" mode where modules are treated as blackboxes. Let me verify quickly... Actually I'll describe it as: `-lib` loads the file as a library of blackbox cells (only port declarations are kept, the implementation is discarded), which is how yosys learns the names/ports of the target cells for `abc`/`dfflibmap` to map into. This is accurate.

Wait — actually for `abc -liberty` and `dfflibmap -liberty`, they read the `.lib` file directly, not `cmos_cells.v`. The `read_verilog -lib cmos_cells.v` loads simulation cell definitions as blackboxes so the netlist has known cell types after mapping. Let me think about what exactly happens.

Actually the purpose of `read_verilog -lib cmos_cells.v` here is to declare the BUF/NOT/NAND/NOR/DFF/DFFSR cells as blackbox modules so that when `abc`/`dfflibmap` map the design into those cell types, yosys recognizes them. Then `write_verilog synth.v` can output a netlist using those cells. The `.lib` file is the actual liberty source for abc/dfflibmap.

Let me describe it carefully: `read_verilog -lib cmos_cells.v` loads the CMOS gate definitions as "lib" (blackbox) cells, registering the cell types (BUF, NOT, NAND, NOR, DFF, DFFSR) and their ports into the design without their implementation. Then `dfflibmap -liberty cmos_cells.lib` and `abc -liberty cmos_cells.lib` map the generic `$dff` and combinational `$`-cells onto the actual liberty cells named in the `.lib`. This is accurate.

Now let me also double-check the prompt format and that `yosys>` is correct. Yes from ShellPass help at yosys.cc:1069.

Let me also verify that I should mention that `synth` is a macro/ScriptPass command (covered in u4-l2). For this beginner lecture, I'll describe `synth` as a "macro command" that runs a whole sequence of sub-commands (proc, opt, memory, techmap, etc.). That's consistent with the example_synth.rst description.

Now let me write the lecture. I need to structure it according to the required format:
1. 本讲目标
2. 前置知识
3. 本讲源码地图
4. 核心概念与源码讲解 (with minimal modules: shell 与命令, 示例工程, help 系统)
5. 综合实践
6. 本讲小结
7. 下一步学习建议

The three minimal modules are:

- 示例工程 (example project)
- help 系统 (help system)

Let me write each module with subsections 4.x.1 (概念说明), 4.x.2 (核心流程), 4.x.3 (源码精读), 4.x.4 (代码实践), 4.x.5 (小练习与答案).

Let me plan the permanent links:
- driver.cc main flow: L449-547 (yosys_setup, run_frontend loop, run_pass, shell/run_backend)

- scripting_intro.rst: L8-50 (script format) and others

Permalink base: https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys/blob/45ea2b8d6c6e94b06ff39b0117f0961ae5c16561/

Format: [文件路径:L起始-L结束](<permalink_base>/<相对路径>#L起始-L结束)

Let me now construct the lecture. I want to make it thorough but readable. Let me write it.

For the practice task, I'll structure it around running counter.ys and tracking cell counts per stage. Since I can't run it, I'll mark certain specifics as "待本地验证" where appropriate, but I can describe what should be observed based on my understanding of the flow.

Let me think about what cell count progression would look like for this counter:
- After read_verilog: process-based (count is reg with always block → RTLIL::Process). No logic cells yet, just the module with a process.
- After synth: the process is converted to $mux/$adff etc. plus $add (for count+1), $eq maybe. Actually for `count + 3'd1` that's an $add. For `if (rst) ... else if (en)` that's mux logic. The synth macro does proc → opt → memory → techmap → ... → ends with gate-level-ish $ cells but NOT mapped to a liberty library (synth alone keeps generic $ cells, or may do abc to internal $_ gates if no liberty). Actually `synth` without -liberty will techmap down to $_AND_ etc gate-level primitives via the default flow. Hmm, actually `synth` runs `abc` with no liberty by default → maps to AIG/internal gate-level `$_AND_`, `$_NOT_`, etc.
- After dfflibmap -liberty cmos_cells.lib: $dff/$_DFF_* → DFF or DFFSR
- After abc -liberty cmos_cells.lib: combinational logic → BUF/NOT/NAND/NOR
- opt_clean: removes unused

So the final netlist should contain DFF (3 of them for the 3-bit count, or 1 if... no, 3 bits → 3 flip-flops, but they'd map to individual DFF cells), NAND/NOR/NOT/BUF for the increment + mux logic.

I'll describe this progression conceptually and mark exact numbers as "待本地验证".

Actually, let me reconsider. The count is 3 bits. After mapping to DFF cells (1-bit each in the lib), there'd be 3 DFF cells. The combinational logic: count+1 increment and the reset/enable mux. With NAND/NOR/NOT only, that's several gates.

I'll keep the practice observational and not claim exact numbers.

Let me write the file now. I'll make it comprehensive and well-structured.

One more thing — let me verify the prompt format detail. In the shell, the prompt is created by `create_prompt(design, recursion_counter)`. From ShellPass help: `yosys>` (whole design), `yosys*>` (partial selection), `yosys [modname]>` (module selected). Good.

Let me also reference the `read_verilog -lib` and the auto frontend detection. Good.

Now writing the lecture. I'll be careful with the permalink format — note the instructions say:
